question_text: Explain, using the particle model, why a gas always fills the entire volume of its container, whereas a liquid always has a fixed volume but takes the shape of its container.
correct_answer: In a gas, particles have very weak or no forces between them and move rapidly in all directions at high speeds, so they spread throughout the entire container. In a liquid, particles have stronger attractive forces that hold them together, so they maintain a fixed volume, but the particles can still move and flow, allowing the liquid to take the shape of its container.
explanation: The key difference is the strength of intermolecular forces: gases have negligible forces, so particles move freely everywhere; liquids have moderate forces that keep the volume constant but allow particles to rearrange.
